title: Individual Oral — presentation and conversation
summary: This lesson provides an in-depth guide to the IB Spanish B SL Individual Oral (IO). It deconstructs the assessment criteria, offers strategies for selecting and analysing a visual stimulus, structuring the presentation, and excelling in the follow-up conversation to achieve top-band marks.
learningObjectives: Understand and apply the four assessment criteria for the Spanish B SL Individual Oral.
Develop a systematic approach to selecting a visual stimulus and connecting it to an IB theme and a specific Spanish-speaking culture.
Master a high-level structure for the 3-4 minute presentation, balancing description, analysis, and personal reflection.
Learn strategies to confidently and fluently engage in the follow-up conversation, providing developed and well-supported responses.
Incorporate sophisticated language, grammatical structures, and discourse markers to meet the demands of Criterion A (Language).

simpleExplanation.title: Your Spanish Story: Mastering the Individual Oral
simpleExplanation.summary: The Individual Oral is a one-on-one conversation with your teacher based on a visual stimulus you analyse. You will deliver a short presentation connecting the image to an IB theme and a Spanish-speaking culture, followed by a deeper discussion on the topic.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of the IO as being a specialist guide for a single, powerful photograph in a gallery. First, you give a prepared but engaging talk (the presentation) explaining the photo's context, what it depicts, and its deeper cultural meaning. Afterwards, an interested visitor (your teacher) asks you follow-up questions to explore your expert insights further (the conversation). Your goal is to be a knowledgeable, articulate, and engaging guide.
steps: Select & Analyse: Choose a rich visual stimulus (photo, cartoon) that clearly links to an IB theme and a specific aspect of a Spanish-speaking culture.
Structure & Outline: Create a bullet-point outline for your 3-4 minute presentation covering description, interpretation, and cultural connection. Do not write a full script.
Practise & Time: Rehearse your presentation to ensure it flows naturally, meets the time limit, and allows you to use complex language with confidence.
Anticipate & Expand: Predict potential follow-up questions. Prepare to discuss the topic broadly, offering justified opinions and examples that go beyond the image itself.

flashcards.back: The visual stimulus (e.g., photograph, advertisement, cartoon) that forms the basis of your presentation.
Criterion A: Language. Assesses the accuracy and range of your grammar and vocabulary, as well as your pronunciation and intonation.

flashcards.front: Criterio B1: Mensaje - Estímulo visual (6 puntos)
flashcards.back: Criterion B1: Message - Visual stimulus. Assesses the relevance, clarity, and development of your ideas during the presentation, and your link to a target culture.
Criterion B2: Message - Conversation. Assesses the depth and development of your responses to the teacher's questions in the follow-up discussion.
Criterion B3: Communication. Assesses your ability to interact effectively, your fluency, and your capacity to maintain a conversation.
The specific connection you make between the image and a Spanish-speaking culture (e.g., 'La siesta in Spain', 'The role of 'quinceañeras' in Mexico').
Discourse markers. Words or phrases that link ideas, such as 'sin embargo' (however), 'por lo tanto' (therefore), 'además' (furthermore).
Filler words. Words like 'este...', 'pues...', 'bueno...' that should be used sparingly to avoid sounding hesitant.
Description is what you literally see ('Hay tres personas'). Interpretation is what it means or symbolises ('Esto representa la importancia de la familia').
A justified opinion. You must support your views with reasons, examples, or evidence ('En mi opinión... porque...').
Relevance. Ensuring everything you say is directly connected to the visual stimulus and the chosen theme.
Development of ideas. Moving beyond superficial statements to provide depth, detail, and nuance in your analysis and responses.

faq.q: What if I don't understand the teacher's question?
faq.a: It is perfectly acceptable to ask for clarification. It shows you are engaged in the conversation. You can say, '¿Podría repetir la pregunta, por favor?' (Could you repeat the question, please?) or 'No entendí bien la pregunta, ¿podría reformularla?' (I didn't quite understand the question, could you rephrase it?).

faq.q: What happens if I make a grammar mistake?
faq.a: Don't panic. The examiner is assessing your overall command of the language, not isolated errors. If you notice the mistake, you can quickly correct it, but the most important thing is to maintain fluency and keep communicating your ideas. A few errors will not prevent you from getting a high score in Criterion A if your language is otherwise rich and complex.

faq.q: How 'specific' does my cultural link need to be?
faq.a: The more specific, the better. Specificity demonstrates genuine engagement and research. Instead of saying 'This is a tradition in Spain', try 'This tradition of 'las doce uvas' on New Year's Eve is a key part of Spanish culture'. This level of detail is what examiners reward in Criterion B1.

faq.q: Can I bring a full script into the exam?
faq.a: No. You are only allowed to bring in a maximum of 10 bullet points on a single sheet of paper. Writing a full script is penalised because it leads to a stilted, unnatural delivery, which will lower your score in Criterion B3 (Communication). The goal is a conversation, not a recitation.

sections.content: The Spanish B SL Individual Oral is your opportunity to showcase your linguistic skills and cultural understanding in a dynamic, interactive format. It accounts for 25% of your final grade. This lesson will guide you through each component, from preparation to performance, equipping you with the strategies to excel and demonstrate the full extent of your abilities.
Understanding what examiners are looking for is the first step to success. The IO is marked against four distinct criteria, totalling 30 marks.

items: **Criterion A: Language (12 marks):** This is about your command of Spanish. To score highly, you must demonstrate a good range of vocabulary and complex grammatical structures (e.g., different tenses, subjunctive mood, conditional clauses) with a high degree of accuracy. Pronunciation and intonation should be clear and help convey meaning.

**Criterion B1: Message - Visual stimulus (6 marks):** This assesses your 3-4 minute presentation. Your ideas must be relevant to the stimulus, well-developed, and clearly organised. A crucial element is making a clear and specific link to a Spanish-speaking culture.

**Criterion B2: Message - Conversation (6 marks):** This evaluates your performance in the follow-up conversation. Top marks are awarded for responses that are not just relevant but also developed, sustained, and show critical thinking. You should be able to justify your opinions and elaborate on your points.

**Criterion B3: Communication (6 marks):** This criterion assesses how well you interact. Are you listening to the questions? Is your speech fluent and easy to follow? High-scoring students engage in a genuine conversation, rather than just delivering pre-rehearsed answers.

sections.content: Choosing and Analysing Your Visual Stimulus

During the 15 minutes of preparation time, you will choose one of two visual stimuli. Your choice is critical. A good stimulus is your launchpad for a great presentation. It should be rich enough to sustain analysis and clearly connect to one of the five prescribed themes: Identidades, Experiencias, Ingenio Humano, Organización Social, or Cómo compartimos el planeta.

items: **Look for Detail and Ambiguity:** Choose an image that has layers of meaning. An image of a crowded street market is better than one of a single apple. It allows for more description and interpretation.
**Ensure a Clear Thematic Link:** You must state which theme the image relates to. Make sure this connection is logical and easy to defend.

**Identify a Specific Cultural Connection:** Avoid generic statements. Instead of 'this relates to Latin America', aim for 'this image of street art in Valparaíso, Chile, reflects the city's bohemian identity...'. Specificity demonstrates engagement and knowledge.
**Consider the 'Story':** What story does the image tell? What happened before? What might happen next? Asking these questions helps you move from simple description to deeper analysis.

sections.question: You have chosen a photograph of young people protesting for climate action in Madrid, holding signs in Spanish. The theme is 'Cómo compartimos el planeta'. Outline your 3-4 minute presentation.
sections.solution:
This model provides a bullet-point outline, which is the recommended preparation method. 

**1. Introducción (aprox. 40 segundos):**
*   **Presentación:** 'He elegido esta imagen que se conecta con el tema de 'Cómo compartimos el planeta' y, más específicamente, con el activismo medioambiental.'
*   **Vínculo cultural:** 'La foto, que parece haber sido tomada en una ciudad española como Madrid o Barcelona, muestra a jóvenes participando en una manifestación por el clima.'
*   **Tesis:** 'Voy a analizar cómo la imagen representa la creciente conciencia y el compromiso de la juventud española con los problemas ecológicos globales.'

**2. Descripción y Análisis (aprox. 1.5 minutos):**
*   **Descripción:** 'En la imagen vemos a un grupo de adolescentes y jóvenes. Llevan pancartas con lemas como 'No hay planeta B'. Sus expresiones son serias y apasionadas. El fondo sugiere una gran ciudad europea.'
*   **Interpretación:** 'Esto no es solo una protesta; es un símbolo del poder colectivo y la urgencia que siente la 'generación Z'. La elección de usar cartón reciclado para las pancartas es en sí misma un mensaje. Simboliza una brecha generacional: los jóvenes exigen acción a los líderes políticos y a las generaciones anteriores.'

**3. Conexión Cultural y Reflexión (aprox. 1.5 minutos):**
*   **Conexión específica:** 'En España, el movimiento 'Fridays for Future' ha ganado mucha fuerza. Este activismo juvenil refleja un cambio en la sociedad española, que históricamente ha enfrentado otros desafíos políticos y económicos. Ahora, el medio ambiente es una prioridad clave para los jóvenes.'
*   **Opinión/Reflexión:** 'Personalmente, encuentro esta imagen muy inspiradora. Me hace pensar que, aunque los problemas como el cambio climático son globales, las soluciones empiezan con acciones locales y el compromiso personal. En mi opinión, es fundamental que los sistemas educativos fomenten este tipo de conciencia crítica.'
*   **Conclusión:** 'En resumen, esta foto captura un momento importante del activismo juvenil en España y sirve como un poderoso recordatorio de nuestra responsabilidad compartida por el planeta.'

sections.content: After your presentation, the teacher will lead a conversation for 4-5 minutes. This is not an interrogation. It is a chance to elaborate on your ideas, demonstrate your ability to think on your feet, and engage in a natural, spontaneous discussion. The questions will flow from what you said in your presentation to broader aspects of the theme.

Do not give short, closed answers. If the teacher asks, '¿Crees que las protestas son efectivas?', don't just say 'Sí'. Expand your answer: 'Sí, creo que pueden ser muy efectivas, aunque su éxito depende de varios factores. Por ejemplo, una protesta puede aumentar la conciencia pública sobre un problema. Sin embargo, para lograr un cambio real, a menudo se necesita también una acción política sostenida.'

sections.question: Following the presentation on the climate protest in Madrid, the teacher asks: 'Mencionaste la responsabilidad. ¿De quién es la responsabilidad principal de solucionar el cambio climático: de los individuos, de las empresas o de los gobiernos?' (You mentioned responsibility. Whose main responsibility is it to solve climate change: individuals, companies, or governments?)
sections.solution:
This model response demonstrates nuance and structured argumentation, key for a high score in Criterion B2 (Message - Conversation).

**Student's Response:** 'Esa es una pregunta compleja y muy importante. No creo que haya un solo responsable. Es una responsabilidad compartida, pero con diferentes niveles de poder e impacto. 

Por un lado, los gobiernos tienen el mayor poder para crear cambios a gran escala. Pueden implementar leyes, como impuestos sobre el carbono o la prohibición de plásticos de un solo uso, que obligan a toda la sociedad a cambiar. 

Por otro lado, las grandes empresas tienen una enorme responsabilidad porque su producción es una de las principales fuentes de contaminación. Deben invertir en tecnologías sostenibles. 

Finalmente, los individuos también tenemos un papel. Nuestras decisiones diarias, como qué compramos o cómo viajamos, suman. Sin embargo, sería injusto poner toda la carga sobre los individuos si las opciones sostenibles no son accesibles o asequibles. 

En conclusión, diría que los gobiernos deben liderar y crear el marco, las empresas deben transformarse y los individuos deben presionar y participar. Es un sistema interconectado.'

**Why this is a top-band response:** It directly answers the question, avoids a simplistic 'one or the other' answer, uses discourse markers ('Por un lado', 'Finalmente', 'En conclusión'), provides specific examples ('impuestos sobre el carbono'), and shows sophisticated, structured thinking.

sections.content: To move into the top band for Criterion A, you need to consciously use a variety of advanced structures. Don't just use the present tense. Show the examiner the breadth of your grammatical knowledge.

items: **Use the Subjunctive:** Essential for expressing opinions, doubts, and hypotheticals. 'No creo que **sea** justo...', 'Es importante que **hagamos** algo...', 'Me gustaría que el gobierno **implementara** más políticas...'
**Employ the Conditional:** Perfect for speculating and giving advice. 'Si yo fuera presidente, **haría**...', 'Se **podría** argumentar que...'
**Vary your Vocabulary:** Instead of 'muy', use 'sumamente', 'extremadamente'. Instead of 'dice', use 'indica', 'sugiere', 'demuestra', 'refleja'.

**Incorporate Idiomatic Expressions:** Using phrases like 'tener en cuenta' (to take into account), 'jugar un papel' (to play a role), or 'a fin de cuentas' (in the end) will make your Spanish sound more authentic.

**Link Your Ideas:** Use a range of connectors beyond 'y', 'pero', 'porque'. Try 'no obstante' (nevertheless), 'por consiguiente' (consequently), 'mientras que' (whereas), 'es decir' (that is to say).

sections.content: Prepare a small 'cheat sheet' of 5-10 sophisticated phrases or structures you want to use. During your 15-minute preparation, write them on your note sheet. This acts as a reminder to elevate your language during both the presentation and conversation.

sections.label: Test your IO skills with a sample prompt
